heading: Change Storage Capacity on Google Cloud
body:
Google Cloud supports extended storage with the following limitations:

- `M40`: up to 4TB

- `M50`/`M60`: up to 8TB

- `M80+`: up to 14TB

> **Note:** Extended storage is available only for `M40+` clusters. To change your
> cluster's tier, see [Modify a Cluster](modify-cluster).

Unlike AWS (Amazon Web Services), Google Cloud doesn't have volume modification cooldown periods.
Therefore, storage changes can be made immediately without waiting for
previous modifications to complete.

When you increase a cluster's storage capacity, Atlas modifies the
size of the servers' data volumes in a rolling manner with zero
downtime. Atlas increases the storage capacity in place without
copying data or performing an [initial sync](manual/core/replica-set-sync).

However, Google Cloud doesn't allow in-place storage capacity reduction.
If you reduce the storage capacity of a cluster, Atlas
provisions new volumes, and then syncs data from the old to the new
volumes using a logical or file copy based initial sync.

If you enable extended storage, this slows down initial syncs and cross-project
snapshot restores, and can reduce high availability. Enabling extended
storage is a temporary solution for clusters that might require additional
storage capacity in the future. We recommend that you enable [sharding](manual/sharding)
for long-term expanded storage capacity.

Before you apply your storage capacity reduction, the
**Review Changes** page notifies you that Atlas triggers a
rolling restart of your cluster when you make this change.

Atlas has disk capacity limits on single replica sets, scaling up to
4 TB for higher cluster tiers. To expand the total cluster storage
beyond default limits, you can enable extended storage in
the [project settings](project-settings). To accommodate further
scaling in the future, enable [Configure a sharded cluster](cluster-option-sharding)
for long-term expansion.
